data.title: Nextpower raises FY2026 outlook to $3.5B revenue and $4.36 EPS with $500M buyback as JV, product innovation drive growth
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Nextpower Inc. (NXT) Q3 2026

Management View

  • CEO Daniel Shugar highlighted a strong quarter for Nextpower, underscoring "solid operational discipline and execution, increased backlog and continuing focus on customers and innovation." Shugar noted, "Q3 revenue grew 34% year-on-year to $909 million, and adjusted EBITDA increased 15% to $214 million." He announced the completion of the Nextpower Arabia joint venture with Abunayyan Holding, which will supply 2.25 gigawatts of tracking systems to a major solar project and support local manufacturing for up to 12 gigawatts of solar capacity annually.

  • Shugar stated, "We are raising our fiscal 2026 financial outlook." He emphasized the company’s new investment-grade rating, describing it as a "meaningful external validation of our cash predictability, disciplined financial management and the durable business model."

  • President Howard Wenger pointed to "continued strong customer bookings, which drove further backlog growth," and highlighted that "U.S. bookings were up and revenue increased 63% year-over-year." Wenger noted international expansion with "record quarterly bookings and expansion into 2 new countries" in Europe and announced the international launch of the NX Earth Truss foundation solution. He added, "Our business fundamentals remain strong. Demand is healthy. Our backlog is large and growing."

  • CFO Charles Boynton reported, "Q3 was another quarter of strong execution with results that reflected both healthy end market demand and continued discipline across the business." Boynton stated, "We generated GAAP net income of $435 million year-to-date," and emphasized the company’s cash position: "We exited the quarter with $953 million of cash and cash equivalents and no debt." He announced, "the Board authorized a share repurchase program of up to $500 million over 3 years."

Outlook

  • Boynton revealed, "We now expect revenue between $3.425 billion and $3.5 billion, adjusted EBITDA between $810 million and $830 million and adjusted diluted EPS in the range of $4.26 to $4.36." He reiterated, "We continue to expect gross margins to be in the low 30s and operating margins in the low 20s." The updated outlook assumes the "current U.S. policy environment remains intact and permitting processes and time lines will remain consistent with historical levels."

Financial Results

  • The company reported Q3 revenue of $909 million and adjusted EBITDA of $214 million, with an adjusted EBITDA margin of 23%. Year-to-date adjusted EBITDA increased 22% year-over-year. Operating cash flow for the quarter was $123 million, and adjusted free cash flow was $119 million. The business generated $391 million in operating cash flow and $360 million in adjusted free cash flow year-to-date. U.S. revenue comprised 81% of Q3 revenue. Tariff impacts increased to $44 million from $33 million in the prior quarter.

Q&A

  • Philip Shen, ROTH Capital Partners: Asked if bookings cleared $1 billion in Q3 and about tracker vs. non-tracker mix in U.S. revenue. Director Howard Wenger responded that backlog reached a new record and was "one of our stronger quarters that we've had in some time." He noted increasing non-tracker impact on U.S. revenue mix, but did not provide specific figures.

  • Praneeth Satish, Wells Fargo: Asked about permit freeze effects and project pipeline. CEO Shugar and Wenger emphasized that "several projects that are on federal lands...are now moving forward" and that developers are navigating constraints with favorable project velocity.

  • Dimple Gosai, BofA Securities: Asked about attach rates for bundled products and margin uplift. Wenger described "expanding exponentially" pipeline opportunities for eBOS and other products but withheld specific figures. Boynton added, "they're roughly at the corporate average" for gross margin.

  • Brian Lee, Goldman Sachs: Asked about fiscal 2027 outlook and IRA credit timing. Boynton said, "we're not updating or changing that," but affirmed, "we feel really good going into next year."

  • Mark Strouse, JPMorgan: Inquired about expectations for Saudi JV orders and buyback approach. Shugar described "very strong double-digit gigawatt growth" in the region. Boynton confirmed the buyback will start "slow and cautious" but be "more of a formalized program."

  • Dushyant Ailani, Jefferies: Sought detail on Saudi JV timing and margins. Wenger said the JV is "operational" and already delivering; Boynton noted it is structured as a roughly 50-50 JV and will not be consolidated.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was positive, with multiple congratulations on the quarter and focused questions on growth, backlog, and margin expansion. Persistent questions about bookings and backlog size indicated strong investor interest in top-line growth.

  • Management’s sentiment was confident in both prepared remarks and Q&A, emphasizing "solid operational discipline," "record backlog," and "very strong double-digit gigawatt growth." Boynton used the phrase "we feel confident in our ability to deliver sustained growth and profitability."

  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone remained upbeat but was more assertive about strategic execution and financial strength. Analysts’ interest shifted slightly from platform expansion to specifics of JV execution and bundled product adoption.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for FY2026 was raised from a previous revenue range of $3.275 billion–$3.475 billion and adjusted diluted EPS of $4.04–$4.25 to $3.425 billion–$3.5 billion and $4.26–$4.36, respectively.

  • Management’s confidence was bolstered by the launch of the Nextpower Arabia JV and the new share repurchase program. Analysts’ questions in the current quarter focused more on the JV, bundled product attach rates, and buyback strategy, compared to the previous quarter’s focus on new platform product launches and acquisition integration.

  • The company’s operational and cash position improved, with higher cash reserves and a formal investment-grade credit rating achieved this quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited tariffs as a continuing impact, with Boynton reporting, "the tariff impact was $44 million, up from $33 million last quarter." However, he stated the impact is "manageable and largely consistent with our prior expectations."

  • Project timing remains subject to some variability, but Wenger described it as "stable and manageable on a portfolio basis."

  • No specific new business risks were highlighted beyond continued policy and permitting assumptions.

Final Takeaway

Management underscored the company’s strong operational performance, expanding backlog, and robust cash generation, citing increased bookings, successful international expansion through the Nextpower Arabia JV, and innovation in bundled technology solutions. The raised FY2026 outlook, new $500 million share repurchase authorization, and investment-grade credit rating reflect confidence in sustained growth, profitability, and cash flow, while ongoing tariff management and stable project execution support the long-term strategy for shareholder value creation.

data.title: Boron One increases private placement
data.description:
• Boron One (BONE:CA) (ERVFF) recently increased its non-brokered private placement by an additional $100,000, for total gross proceeds of up to $600,000 by the issue of units at a price of $0.05 per unit. 
  • The company intends to use net proceeds for working capital requirements.
  • The company received a filing extension until February 20, 2026, for required documentation with the TSX Venture Exchange.

data.title: Earnings Snapshot: ASML beats Q4 revenue estimates but misses on earnings; initiates Q1 and FY26 outlook
K2 Gold to raise C$15M privately
data.description:
• K2 Gold (KTO:CA) (KTGDF) announced a non-brokered private placement and will issue up to ~21.43M common shares in the capital of the company at a price of C$0.70 per offered share.
  • Total gross proceeds are expected to be C$15M.
  • The net proceeds from the offering are expected to be used for exploration at the Mojave and Si2 projects as well as for general corporate purposes.
  • The closing of the offering is expected to occur as soon as practicable.

data.title: KRAKacquisition prices upsized $300M IPO at $10 per share
data.description:
• KRAKacquisition (KRAQU), a blank-check SPAC backed by Kraken affiliates, priced its upsized IPO at $300M, including 30M units at $10 each, up from an initial 25M, with trading under "KRAQU" on Nasdaq starting January 28, 2026.
  • Each unit comprises one Class A ordinary share and one-fourth of a redeemable warrant, exercisable at $11.50 per share; separate trading of shares (KRAQ) and warrants (KRAQW) is expected later. 
  • The underwriters have a 45-day option to purchase up to 4.5M additional units at IPO price to cover over-allotments, if any.
  • The closing is anticipated on January 29, 2026.

data.title: ASML Holding reports mixed Q4 results; initiates Q1 and FY26 outlook
data.description:
• ASML Holding press release (ASML): Q4 GAAP EPS of €7.35 misses by €0.23.
  • Revenue of €9.72B (+5.0% Y/Y) beats by €140M.
  • Quarterly net bookings in Q4 of €13.2 billion of which €7.4 billion is EUV.
  • Backlog at the end of 2025 of €38.8 billion.
  • ASML expects Q1 2026 total net sales between €8.2 billion and €8.9 billion, and a gross margin between 51% and 53%.
  • ASML expects 2026 total net sales to be between €34 billion and €39 billion, with a gross margin between 51% and 53%.
  • ASML announces a new share buyback program of up to €12 billion to be executed by December 31, 2028.
  • ASML intends to declare a total dividend for the year 2025 of €7.50 per ordinary share, which is a 17% increase compared to 2024.

data.title: Runway Growth Finance prices 7.25% senior notes offering
data.description:
• Runway Growth Finance (RWAY) priced an underwritten public offering of $100M aggregate principal amount of notes due 2031.​

  • The notes carry a 7.25% annual interest rate, payable quarterly starting March 1, 2026, and mature on February 3, 2031. They can be redeemed at the company's option on or after February 3, 2028, and will be issued in $25 denominations.
  • The underwriters have a 30-day option to purchase up to $15M more to cover overallotments.
  • Net proceeds are expected to be $97M.
  • The offering is expected to close on February 3, 2026.
  • The funds will be used to repay existing debt, including all 8.00% notes due 2027 ($51.75M outstanding as of January 23, 2026) and part of the 7.50% notes due 2027 ($80.5M outstanding), plus general corporate purposes.

data.title: Pony.ai partners with ATBB for robotaxi expansion in China
LG Display reports Q4 results
data.description:
• LG Display press release (LPL): Q4 EBITDA in the fourth quarter of 2025 was KRW 1,162 billion, compared with EBITDA of KRW 1,424 billion in the third quarter of 2025 and KRW 1,306 billion in the fourth quarter of 2024.
  • Net loss in the fourth quarter of 2025 was KRW 351 billion, compared with the net profit of 1 billion in the third quarter of 2025 and with the net loss of KRW 839 billion in the fourth quarter of 2024.
  • Revenue of KRW 7201B (-8.1% Y/Y).

  • OLED products accounted for a record-high 61% of total revenues in 2025. The OLED revenue share has shown consistent growth, rising from 32% in 2020, to 40% in 2022, and 55% in 2024. In 2025, LG Display’s exit from the large-sized LCD business further accelerated its strategic shift from LCD to OLED.

data.title: Coronado outlines $200M–$250M 2026 liquidity uplift amid cost reductions and production expansion
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Coronado Global Resources Inc. (CODQL) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Douglas Thompson opened the call by referencing the completion of a multiyear improvement plan, stating the company has “sustainably lowered our cost base, increased production across both regions and stabilized operating assets and expanded our capacity in line with our long-term strategy.” He emphasized that Coronado is now “more predictable, more resilient and better positioned to leverage improving market conditions.”

  • Thompson reported solid production of 16 million tonnes for 2025, a 4% year-on-year increase, at the lower end of guidance. He highlighted an 11% quarter-on-quarter increase in sales volumes, the highest quarterly sales since Q3 2021, and a $300 million reduction in operating costs for the year. The Buchanan expansion and Mammoth Underground achieved expected run rates in the second half, while Curragh’s second-half saleable production averaged 36% higher than the first half.

  • Strategic liquidity actions included $150 million in support from Stanwell in mid-2025 and a subsequent transaction providing liquidity mechanisms for 2026. Thompson stated, “In 2026 Stanwell’s mechanisms are expected to provide between $200 million and $250 million of cash flow uplift depending on prices and nominated tonnages.” The company secured a 5-year $265 million ABL facility at 9%, fully drawn in December.

  • CFO Barend Van Der Merwe stated, “In FY '25, we achieved approximately USD 300 million in operating cost reductions versus the prior year.” He noted average mining cost of $97.6/tonne, down from $107/tonne in FY '24. He added, “We closed December with $173 million of cash and did not do any liquidity management or working capital management levers at the end of FY '25.”

Outlook

  • Coronado expects to “lift production rates in 2026, supported by our expansion projects.” Management forecast cash flow uplift from the Stanwell agreement to be “between $200 million and $250 million” in 2026, with price and cash flow downside protected by the agreement.

  • Planned capital expenditures are set to be lower in FY '26, as the major investment phase concludes.

  • Management stated, “We’ll be releasing our financial results for 2025 and 2026 guidance to the market on 24 February 2026.”

Financial Results

  • Production for 2025 was reported at 16 million tonnes, a 4% year-on-year increase. The group’s average mining cost per tonne sold was $97, a 10% reduction year-on-year.

  • CapEx for the year totaled $245 million, at the bottom end of guidance, and $38 million for the December quarter.

  • Coronado ended 2025 with $173 million in cash and no near-term debt maturities. The company fully drew its $265 million ABL facility and repaid the Oaktree credit facility in December.

  • Buchanan’s December alone generated approximately 400,000 tonnes of saleable production, $20 million of earnings, and a $67/tonne unit rate.

Q&A

  • Robert Stein, Macquarie Research, asked about the recent fatalities and operational risk at Mammoth. CEO Thompson responded, “With the incident that's occurred here in Australia, investigations are ongoing... all efforts have been made by us as demonstrated in the past that these tragic events get the benefit of the full focus of the business and all the learnings that we can get from it are shared with the industry as quickly as possible.”

  • Glyn Lawcock, Barrenjoey, inquired about the timeline for regulator decisions at Mammoth and Curragh’s rehabilitation fund. Thompson replied that the Section 201 Investigation Report is due within 20 days, and the rehabilitation fund determination is expected in February.

  • Daniel Roden, Jefferies, questioned the impact of Cyclone Koji and working capital. Thompson explained the company’s resilience due to inventory and improved mining methods, while Van Der Merwe confirmed, “the $173 million at the end of December, is real cash. It's available. There's nothing to be called up from that.”

  • Chen Jiang, BofA Securities, asked about U.S. domestic coal sales and minority asset disposal. Thompson said negotiations are ongoing and that the board has mandated continued exploration of minority sales, with strong interest and ongoing discussions.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was neutral to slightly cautious, focused on operational risks, regulatory processes, working capital, and deleveraging. They probed on incident impacts, asset sales, and debt strategy, reflecting careful scrutiny.

  • Management’s tone in prepared remarks was confident and assertive, especially regarding cost reductions and improved stability, but shifted to more measured and cautious during Q&A, particularly on regulatory and safety matters. Thompson emphasized, “I do not see that kind of change being material to the way in which we think about Logan's operations into the future.”

  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone is more optimistic due to improved cost structure and liquidity, while analysts remain focused on risk and execution.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language has shifted from cautious optimism in Q3 to clear articulation of cost reductions and production growth for FY '26 in Q4.

  • Strategic focus moved from ramp-up and project completion in Q3 to maximizing margin from a steady-state, low-cost base and leveraging the Stanwell agreement in Q4.

  • Analysts continued to press on operational execution, regulatory risk, and financial strategy, but Q4 saw increased attention to asset sales and deleveraging.

  • Key metrics improved: mining cost per tonne sold fell from $107/tonne in FY '24 to $97/tonne in FY '25, and production increased year-on-year.

  • Management’s confidence increased in Q4, while analysts’ tone remained probing but less focused on acute liquidity risk than in Q3.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged two recent fatalities, with ongoing investigations and regulatory processes at Mammoth. Thompson assured all learnings will be shared and operations will resume safely once cleared.

  • Uncertainty remains around the timeline for regulatory decisions at Mammoth and regarding rehabilitation fund requirements at Curragh, with February indicated as a potential resolution month.

  • The high-vol met coal market in the U.S. presents structural challenges for Logan, with management reviewing strategic options and portfolio allocation.

  • Potential minority asset disposals are under consideration to accelerate deleveraging, with ongoing negotiations and strong interest reported.

Final Takeaway

Coronado Global Resources delivered significant cost reductions, production growth, and enhanced liquidity in Q4 2025, supported by the completion of major expansion projects and strategic agreements with Stanwell. Management signals a focus on margin optimization and deleveraging in 2026, with increased resilience to market and operational shocks. While regulatory and safety investigations present ongoing risks, the company enters the new year with a structurally lower cost base, improved cash position, and flexibility to pursue further strategic actions.
